title: The IQR Method (Box Plots)
concept: The IQR method uses the middle 50% spread of data to calculate boundary fences, flagging anything beyond 1.5 times the IQR as an outlier.

exercises.prompt: Warmup: Why is the Z-score method unreliable for detecting outliers in highly skewed data distributions?

options.text: Because the mean and standard deviation are themselves heavily distorted by the outliers
Because Z-score only works with text data
Because standard deviations cannot be computed for non-integer numbers

exercises.explain: Yes! Outliers pull the mean and inflate the standard deviation, which hides the outliers from the Z-score. We need a method based on the median instead.

cards.title: Sorting apples
cards.body: Imagine checking the size of apples on a farm. To ignore abnormally tiny or giant ones, you can look at the typical spread of the middle 50% of the harvest to set normal limits.

cards.title: The IQR Method
cards.body: The Interquartile Range (IQR) measures the spread of the middle 50% of your data. It is calculated by subtracting the 25th percentile (Q1) from the 75th percentile (Q3).

cards.title: Building fences
cards.body: Imagine drawing a box around the middle half of your data. The width of this box is the IQR. To find outliers, we build 'fences' 1.5 times the box width away from both ends.

cards.title: Calculating boundaries
cards.body: If Q1 of test scores is 60 and Q3 is 80, the IQR is 20. The upper fence is Q3 + (1.5 × 20) = 110. The lower fence is Q1 - (1.5 × 20) = 30. Any score outside [30, 110] is an outlier.

cards.title: The 1.5x rule isn't magic
cards.body: Why 1.5? It was proposed by statistician John Tukey as a robust rule of thumb. In a bell curve, it aligns closely with the Z-score threshold, but handles skewed data much better.

cards.title: Box Plot Boundaries
cards.body: How the IQR method partitions data ranges into normal and outliers:

left.text:
Middle 50% of data
Width = IQR
Normal data core

right.text:
Outlier region
Too far from Q1 or Q3
Flagged for inspection

exercises.prompt: If the 25th percentile (Q1) is 10 and the 75th percentile (Q3) is 30, what is the Interquartile Range (IQR)?
exercises.explain: The IQR is calculated by subtracting Q1 from Q3: 30 - 10 = 20.

exercises.prompt: True or false: The IQR method is less affected by extreme outliers than the Z-score method.
exercises.explain: True! Since the IQR relies on percentiles (Q1 and Q3) rather than the mean, extreme outliers do not distort the boundaries.

exercises.sentence: The IQR method flags outliers by placing fences that are ___ times the IQR away from Q1 and Q3.
exercises.explain: The standard convention is to place outlier fences 1.5 times the IQR below Q1 and above Q3.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following describes the mathematical upper outlier fence in the IQR method?
exercises.explain: The upper fence is Q3 + 1.5 * IQR. Any data point higher than this value is flagged as an outlier.

exercises.prompt: You analyze housing prices where Q1 is $200k, Q3 is $400k, and a luxury home is listed at $800k. Using the IQR method, what are the upper fence and outlier status of the luxury home?

options.text: Upper fence is $700k. The home is an outlier because $800k is greater than $700k.
Upper fence is $600k. The home is not an outlier.
Upper fence is $1.1 million. The home is normal.
Upper fence is $400k. The home is an outlier.

exercises.explain: Correct! The IQR is $400k - $200k = $200k. The upper fence is Q3 + (1.5 * IQR) = $400k + (1.5 * $200k) = $400k + $300k = $700k. Since $800k is greater than $700k, it is an outlier. Incredible job mastering the IQR method!
